note_data.title: Ecology
keyPoints: All living organisms undergo seven life processes: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, and Nutrition
Classification groups organisms by shared features, with species defined as organisms that can reproduce to produce fertile offspring
The binomial naming system uses two parts showing genus and species, such as Homo sapiens for humans
Organisms are classified by evolutionary relationships found through studying physical characteristics and DNA base sequences
The five kingdoms are Animal, Plant, Fungus, Prokaryote and Protoctist, categorized using cellular features
Vertebrates contain a backbone while arthropods have an exoskeleton and segmented body
Plants are divided into flowering and non-flowering, with flowering plants further split into dicotyledons and monocotyledons
Viruses are not classified as living as they cannot complete life processes and are non-cellular with genetic material surrounded by a protein coat
Pathogens are organisms that cause transmissible diseases and include bacteria and viruses
Pathogens transmit through direct contact via body fluids or indirectly through contaminated surfaces, foods, animals and air

definitions.definition: A group of organisms that can reproduce to produce fertile offspring
An internationally agreed system where scientific names have two parts showing genus and species
Diseases caused by pathogens that can be passed from host to host
Animals which contain a backbone, such as mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians and fish

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give characteristics or explain what happens in a process step by step
Give a reasonable answer based on your knowledge, use 'may' or 'might'

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the number of organisms decreases at each level in a food chain. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Energy is lost at each trophic level as heat during respiration. Energy is also lost through movement and excretion. Only about 10% of energy is passed to the next level, so there is insufficient energy to support large numbers of organisms at higher levels.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Energy is lost as heat/during respiration
Mark 2: Energy lost through movement/excretion/undigested material
Mark 3: Only 10% energy transferred/insufficient energy for large numbers at higher levels

commonMistakes: Confusing growth with temporary size changes - growth must be a permanent increase in size measured through dry mass, cell size or number of cells
Thinking viruses are living organisms - they are non-cellular and cannot complete life processes independently
Mixing up direct and indirect pathogen transmission - direct involves body fluid transfer while indirect involves contaminated surfaces, foods, animals and air
Assuming physical similarities always indicate close evolutionary relationships - different species in the same habitat often evolve similar traits
Forgetting that arthropods are identified by both exoskeleton AND segmented body, not just one feature
examinerTips: When naming organisms, always write the genus first with a capital letter, then species in lowercase, both italicized or underlined
Remember that DNA sequencing is more accurate than comparing physical characteristics for determining evolutionary relationships
Focus on prevention methods for disease transmission: hygienic food preparation, waste treatment, clean water supply and good personal hygiene
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Food chain diagram showing producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → tertiary consumer with arrows indicating energy transfer direction. Include energy loss at each level (heat, movement, excretion).
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to complete a passage about ecology by filling in 8 blanks with suitable words about organisms, ecosystems, populations, communities, and sampling methods
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify the correct definition of a community from multiple choice options
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe how to use quadrats to estimate the population of dandelions in a field, including sampling method and calculations
This topic was tested in O/N 2021 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference between population and community with examples from a named ecosystem
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — they asked students to calculate population density given quadrat data

workedExample.question: A student uses quadrats to estimate the population of clover plants in a field. She places 10 quadrats randomly and counts the number of clover plants in each quadrat. Her results are: 4, 6, 2, 8, 5, 7, 3, 6, 4, 5. The total area of the field is 200m². Each quadrat has an area of 0.5m². Calculate the estimated total population of clover plants in the field. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Add up all the counts from the 10 quadrats: 4+6+2+8+5+7+3+6+4+5 = 50 plants
Step 2: Calculate the mean number of plants per quadrat: 50 ÷ 10 = 5 plants per quadrat
Step 3: Calculate how many quadrats would fit in the total field area: 200m² ÷ 0.5m² = 400 quadrats
Step 4: Multiply the mean by the total number of possible quadrats: 5 × 400 = 2000 plants
workedExample.answer: Total population estimate = 2000 clover plants in the field
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correctly adding counts (50), 1 mark for calculating mean (5), 1 mark for calculating total quadrats (400), 1 mark for final answer (2000)

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused population and community' — always remember that population refers to ONE species while community refers to ALL species in a habitat
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to mention random sampling when describing quadrat methods' — always state that quadrats must be placed randomly to avoid bias

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often calculated incorrectly when estimating populations from quadrat data' — always show your working: total counts ÷ number of quadrats = mean, then mean × total possible quadrats = population estimate
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a pyramid of numbers showing the relative population sizes at each trophic level. Draw as stacked rectangles with producers at the bottom (widest), primary consumers above (narrower), secondary consumers above that (even narrower), and tertiary consumers at the top (narrowest). Label each level clearly and include examples of organisms at each level. Also be able to sketch quadrat sampling method showing random placement of square frames in a habitat.
note_data.quickRecap: Living organisms are characterized by seven life processes and classified using the binomial system based on evolutionary relationships determined through DNA and physical features. The five kingdoms contain organisms with different cellular features, with animals divided into vertebrates and arthropods, and plants into flowering and non-flowering types. Pathogens cause transmissible diseases through direct or indirect transmission, making hygiene and sanitation crucial for disease prevention.
